chapters.title: Volcanic Eruption
chapters.subtitle: Ash fall, lava flows, pyroclastic flows
chapters.quickFact: Volcanic ash is not soft — it is fine shards of rock and glass that can collapse roofs, contaminate water, and cause lung damage.

tips.title: Know Your Volcano Alert Levels
tips.body: USGS uses a four-level alert system (Normal, Advisory, Watch, Warning). Know what each level means and what actions to take for your volcano.

tips.title: Prepare for Ash Fall
tips.body: Store N95 respirators, safety goggles, and extra air filters. Ash fall can affect areas hundreds of miles from the eruption.

tips.title: Know Lahar and Flow Hazard Zones
tips.body: Lava flows and lahars (volcanic mudflows) follow valleys and river channels. Know if your home is in a mapped flow zone.

tips.title: Stock Supplies for Shelter-in-Place
tips.body: Volcanic events can last days to months. Maintain a 2-week supply of food, water, medications, and essential items.

tips.title: Protect Mechanical Equipment
tips.body: Volcanic ash clogs engines, HVAC systems, and electronics. Cover or store outdoor equipment and spare air filters for vehicles.

tips.title: Evacuate Lava Flow Zones Immediately
tips.body: Lava flows and pyroclastic flows cannot be outrun on foot. Evacuate immediately when instructed. Do not return to retrieve belongings.

tips.title: Shelter from Ash Fall
tips.body: Go indoors, close all windows, doors, and dampers, and turn off HVAC systems. Seal gaps with wet towels to reduce ash intrusion.

tips.title: Wear Respiratory and Eye Protection
tips.body: If you must go outside during ash fall, wear an N95 mask and goggles. Volcanic ash damages eyes and lungs rapidly.

tips.title: Clear Ash from Rooftops
tips.body: Volcanic ash is extremely heavy when wet. Clear it from flat roofs frequently to prevent collapse.

tips.title: Avoid River Valleys During Heavy Rain
tips.body: Lahars can travel rapidly down river valleys during or after eruptions. Do not shelter in low-lying river areas.

tips.title: Return Only When Declared Safe
tips.body: Volcanic hazards persist long after the initial eruption. Return only after USGS and local authorities confirm it is safe.

tips.title: Do Not Drink Tap Water Without Testing
tips.body: Ash can contaminate water supplies with fluoride and other chemicals. Do not drink tap water until authorities confirm it is safe.

tips.title: Clean Ash Carefully
tips.body: Dampen ash before sweeping to prevent re-suspension. Never use a dry broom — this drives toxic particles into the air.

tips.title: Protect Your Lungs During Cleanup
tips.body: Continue wearing an N95 respirator during all ash cleanup. Silica particles in ash cause long-term lung disease with prolonged exposure.

tips.title: Monitor Structural Integrity
tips.body: Ash-loaded structures and those near lava flows may have hidden damage. Have your home professionally inspected before full re-occupancy.

chapters.title: Earthquake
chapters.subtitle: Ground shaking, structural collapse
chapters.quickFact: Most earthquake injuries occur from falling objects and debris — not the shaking itself.

tips.title: Secure Heavy Furniture
tips.body: Bolt bookcases, water heaters, and large appliances to walls. Unsecured furniture is a leading cause of earthquake injuries.

tips.title: Identify Safe Spots
tips.body: In each room, identify places to Drop, Cover, and Hold On — under sturdy tables or against interior walls away from windows.

tips.title: Prepare an Emergency Kit
tips.body: Keep at least 72 hours of water (1 gallon/person/day), non-perishable food, first-aid kit, flashlight, and batteries ready.

tips.title: Know How to Shut Off Utilities
tips.body: Learn to shut off gas, water, and electricity at main valves. Post instructions near each shut-off point.

tips.title: Practice Drop, Cover, Hold On
tips.body: Drill the earthquake response with your household at least twice a year so the action becomes instinctive.

tips.title: Drop, Cover, and Hold On
tips.body: Immediately drop to hands and knees, take cover under a sturdy desk or table, and hold on until shaking stops.

tips.title: Stay Indoors
tips.body: Do not run outside — most injuries happen near building exits from falling debris. Stay where you are until shaking stops.

tips.title: Stay Away from Windows
tips.body: Move away from glass, windows, outside doors, and walls. Broken glass is a major injury source during shaking.

tips.title: If in Bed, Stay There
tips.body: Hold on and protect your head with a pillow. Moving during shaking increases injury risk from falls.

tips.title: If Outdoors, Move to Open Space
tips.body: Stay away from buildings, streetlights, and overhead utility lines. Crouch low until shaking stops.

tips.title: Check for Gas Leaks
tips.body: If you smell gas or hear hissing, open windows, leave the building, and do not use any electrical switches. Call the gas company from outside.

tips.title: Inspect for Structural Damage
tips.body: Do not re-enter damaged buildings. Look for cracks in foundations, leaning walls, or broken supports before assuming a structure is safe.

tips.title: Expect Aftershocks
tips.body: Aftershocks can follow the main quake for hours or days. Drop, cover, and hold on when you feel them.

tips.title: Use Texts Instead of Calls
tips.body: Phone networks become congested after earthquakes. Text messages and data use less network bandwidth than voice calls.

tips.title: Document Damage for Insurance
tips.body: Photograph all visible damage to your property before cleanup begins. This is essential for insurance claims.

chapters.title: Hurricane
chapters.subtitle: Storm surge, wind, flooding
chapters.quickFact: Storm surge — not wind — is the deadliest aspect of a hurricane, capable of pushing 20+ feet of water inland.

tips.title: Know Your Evacuation Zone
tips.body: Find out if your home is in a hurricane evacuation zone. Know at least two exit routes from your area and have a destination in mind.

tips.title: Board Up Windows
tips.body: Install storm shutters or board windows with 5/8-inch plywood cut to fit. Tape does not prevent breakage.

tips.title: Fill Up on Fuel
tips.body: Keep vehicle tanks full before the storm — gas stations may lose power or run out of fuel during evacuations.

tips.title: Store Important Documents
tips.body: Place insurance policies, IDs, and financial records in a waterproof container. Consider digital copies in cloud storage.

tips.title: Bring Outdoor Objects Indoors
tips.body: Secure or bring inside lawn furniture, decorations, garbage cans, and anything else that wind can turn into a projectile.

tips.title: Stay Indoors and Away from Windows
tips.body: Remain in an interior room on the lowest floor. Avoid windows and glass doors throughout the storm.

tips.title: Do Not Go Out in the Eye
tips.body: When the eye passes, winds will calm temporarily. Do not go outside — the other eyewall will arrive with equal fury within minutes.

tips.title: Monitor Emergency Broadcasts
tips.body: Use a battery-powered or hand-crank radio to monitor NOAA weather radio and local emergency broadcasts.

tips.title: Turn Off Utilities if Instructed
tips.body: Shut off utilities at main switches if local authorities instruct you to do so or if your home sustains structural damage.

tips.title: Fill Bathtubs with Water
tips.body: Fill bathtubs and large containers with water before the storm. Water supply may become contaminated or cut off.

tips.title: Avoid Floodwater
tips.body: Do not walk or drive through floodwater. Just 6 inches of fast-moving water can knock a person down; 12 inches can sweep away a car.

tips.title: Watch for Downed Power Lines
tips.body: Stay away from downed power lines and standing water near them. Report them to the utility company immediately.

tips.title: Return Only When Declared Safe
tips.body: Do not return to evacuated areas until local officials declare it safe. Roads may be blocked or structurally compromised.

tips.title: Use Generators Safely
tips.body: Run generators outdoors at least 20 feet from windows and doors. Carbon monoxide from generators kills dozens each year after storms.

tips.title: Discard Flood-Contaminated Food
tips.body: Throw away any food that came into contact with floodwater. When in doubt, throw it out — foodborne illness risk is high.

chapters.title: Wildfire
chapters.subtitle: Fast-moving flames, toxic smoke
chapters.quickFact: Wildfires can travel at up to 14 miles per hour and change direction within seconds in shifting winds.

tips.title: Create Defensible Space
tips.body: Clear flammable vegetation and debris within 30 feet of your home. Extend to 100 feet using reduced-density planting to slow fire spread.

tips.title: Prepare a Go-Bag
tips.body: Keep a pre-packed bag ready with medications, IDs, chargers, cash, and a change of clothes. You may have only minutes to evacuate.

tips.title: Use Fire-Resistant Materials
tips.body: Install ember-resistant vents, use Class A roof materials, and seal gaps in eaves. Most homes ignite from embers, not direct flames.

tips.title: Sign Up for Local Alerts
tips.body: Register for your county's emergency notification system. Evacuation orders are issued via these channels first.

tips.title: Know Multiple Evacuation Routes
tips.body: Identify at least two exits from your neighborhood. Roads can become blocked by fire or traffic during mass evacuations.

tips.title: Evacuate Early
tips.body: Do not wait until you see flames or receive a mandatory order. Leaving early keeps roads clear and keeps you safe.

tips.title: Close All Windows and Doors
tips.body: If you must shelter in place, close all windows, doors, and fireplace dampers. Sealing the home slows smoke and ember intrusion.

tips.title: Wear Protective Clothing
tips.body: Put on long pants, long sleeves, leather or heavy shoes, and goggles. Use an N95 mask to filter smoke particles.

tips.title: Fill Sinks and Tubs with Water
tips.body: Fill any large containers with water. This provides a last-resort water source for firefighting or cooling burned areas.

tips.title: Turn On All Interior Lights
tips.body: Making your house bright helps firefighters see it through dense smoke.

tips.title: Watch for Hot Spots
tips.body: Do not assume the fire is out. Smoldering roots and debris can reignite. Walk the perimeter of your property with water ready.

tips.title: Avoid Ash and Debris
tips.body: Wildfire ash may contain toxic chemicals. Wear gloves, long sleeves, and an N95 mask when cleaning up.

tips.title: Check for Structural Damage
tips.body: Fire can damage structural integrity without visible signs. Have a professional inspect your home before returning to normal use.

tips.title: Watch for Debris Flows
tips.body: Burned hillsides lose soil stability. Heavy rain after a wildfire can trigger flash flooding and mudslides. Monitor weather closely.

tips.title: Seek Mental Health Support
tips.body: Wildfire trauma is real. Contact local disaster mental health services or the SAMHSA helpline if you feel overwhelmed.

chapters.title: Flood
chapters.subtitle: Rising water, contamination, currents
chapters.quickFact: Floods are the most common and costly natural disaster in the US — just 1 inch of water can cause $25,000 in damage.

tips.title: Know Your Flood Risk
tips.body: Check FEMA's Flood Map Service Center to see if your home is in a flood zone. Understand your flood risk level.

tips.title: Purchase Flood Insurance
tips.body: Standard homeowner's insurance does not cover flooding. NFIP flood policies take 30 days to take effect — don't wait.

tips.title: Elevate Electrical Systems
tips.body: Move electrical panels, switches, and appliances at least 12 inches above your area's base flood elevation.

tips.title: Install Backflow Valves
tips.body: Install check valves in plumbing to prevent floodwater from backing up into your drains and toilets.

tips.title: Store Sandbags and Barriers
tips.body: Keep sandbags or water-activated flood barriers available for quick deployment around doors and low openings.

tips.title: Move to Higher Ground Immediately
tips.body: If flooding is imminent, move to upper floors or high ground. Do not wait for official orders if water is rising.

tips.title: Never Drive Through Floodwater
tips.body: Turn Around, Don't Drown. Two feet of water will carry away most vehicles. Roads under water may be washed out.

tips.title: Turn Off Electricity at Breaker
tips.body: If safe to do so, turn off electricity at the main breaker before water reaches electrical outlets. Never touch electrical equipment in water.

tips.title: Disconnect Appliances
tips.body: Unplug appliances while still safe to do so. Move valuables to upper floors if time permits.

tips.title: Avoid Contact with Floodwater
tips.body: Floodwater can contain sewage, chemicals, and pathogens. Avoid contact with skin; wash thoroughly if exposed.

tips.title: Do Not Return Until Safe
tips.body: Wait for official all-clear before returning home. Buildings may be structurally unstable and water may remain contaminated.

tips.title: Document All Damage
tips.body: Photograph and video-record all damage before cleanup. Contact your insurance company immediately to begin the claims process.

tips.title: Dry Out Your Home Quickly
tips.body: Open windows and doors, use fans and dehumidifiers. Mold can begin growing within 24–48 hours of flooding.

tips.title: Disinfect Everything Water Touched
tips.body: Floodwater is contaminated. Clean and disinfect all surfaces, furniture, and belongings that were submerged.

tips.title: Test Well Water Before Use
tips.body: If you have a private well, have it tested before drinking. Floodwater routinely contaminates groundwater supplies.

chapters.title: Tornado
chapters.subtitle: Violent winds, sudden destruction
chapters.quickFact: A tornado can change direction in an instant and travel at speeds of 300+ mph — giving you as little as 13 minutes of warning.

tips.title: Designate a Safe Room
tips.body: Identify the lowest, innermost room in your home — a basement or interior bathroom on the ground floor. This is your tornado shelter.

tips.title: Get a Weather Radio
tips.body: A NOAA weather radio with battery backup will alert you when a tornado watch or warning is issued — even at night.

tips.title: Know the Warning Signs
tips.body: Dark, greenish sky; large hail; a loud roar like a freight train; and a rotating, funnel-shaped cloud all precede tornadoes.

tips.title: Practice Your Response Plan
tips.body: Drill your tornado response with all household members so everyone knows where to go without thinking.

tips.title: Prepare a Tornado Kit
tips.body: Keep helmets, shoes, a first-aid kit, water, and a battery radio in your safe room. Tornadoes often strike at night.

tips.title: Go to Your Safe Room Immediately
tips.body: At the first warning, go to your basement or interior ground-floor room. Get under a staircase or heavy furniture and cover your head.

tips.title: Never Shelter in a Mobile Home
tips.body: Mobile and manufactured homes offer no protection. Go to a nearby sturdy building or a designated community shelter immediately.

tips.title: Stay Away from Windows
tips.body: Flying glass and debris cause most tornado injuries. Keep away from all windows, even to look at the storm.

tips.title: If Outdoors, Lie in a Ditch
tips.body: If caught outside with no shelter, lie flat in a low-lying ditch, cover your head with your hands, and avoid trees and cars.

tips.title: Do Not Shelter Under Bridges
tips.body: Highway overpasses funnel and amplify wind speeds. They are not safe shelters — you have more exposure there than in the open.

tips.title: Watch for Structural Damage
tips.body: Tornadoes can compromise structures without visible destruction. Approach damaged buildings carefully and check for gas leaks before entering.

tips.title: Account for All Household Members
tips.body: Check on all household members immediately. Contact local emergency services if anyone is missing or injured.

tips.title: Wear Protective Gear During Cleanup
tips.body: Wear heavy gloves, sturdy boots, and long sleeves when clearing debris. Nails, glass, and sharp metal are common hazards.

tips.title: Avoid Downed Power Lines
tips.body: Treat all downed lines as live. Stay at least 30 feet away and call your utility company immediately.

tips.title: Register with Disaster Relief Services
tips.body: Register with local emergency management to access FEMA assistance, temporary housing, food, and financial aid.

chapters.title: Tsunami
chapters.subtitle: Ocean waves, coastal flooding
chapters.quickFact: A tsunami can travel across an ocean at 500 mph and arrive as a series of waves for several hours after the first.

tips.title: Know Tsunami Hazard Zones
tips.body: Identify if your home or workplace is in a designated tsunami inundation zone. Know the nearest high ground and how to reach it on foot.

tips.title: Plan an Evacuation Route to High Ground
tips.body: Map out routes to elevated areas at least 100 feet above sea level or 2 miles inland. Walk the route to know how long it takes.

tips.title: Learn the Natural Warning Signs
tips.body: A strong or long earthquake near the coast, a sudden receding of ocean water (draw-back), or a loud ocean roar are all tsunami warnings.

tips.title: Sign Up for Tsunami Alerts
tips.body: Register for Wireless Emergency Alerts and NOAA tsunami warnings for your area. These provide critical minutes of advance notice.

tips.title: Practice Vertical Evacuation
tips.body: If you cannot reach high ground in time, a tall, reinforced concrete building may provide vertical evacuation. Know these structures near you.

tips.title: If You Feel a Quake, Move Inland Immediately
tips.body: Do not wait for an official warning. A strong coastal earthquake is your signal. Begin moving to high ground right away.

tips.title: Never Go to the Shore to Watch
tips.body: The draw-back of water before a tsunami may look dramatic, but going to look puts you in the direct path of the oncoming wave.

tips.title: Abandon Your Car if Needed
tips.body: If traffic prevents escape by vehicle, leave your car and move on foot. You can move faster on foot in traffic or debris.

tips.title: Climb as High as Possible
tips.body: Get to the highest point you can reach. Tsunami waves can exceed 100 feet in height near the source and still be 20–30 feet at shore.

tips.title: Stay Out of Floodwater
tips.body: Tsunami floodwater carries debris and strong currents. Even shallow water can sweep you off your feet.

tips.title: Wait for the All-Clear
tips.body: Do not return to coastal areas until officials declare it safe. Tsunamis arrive in multiple waves; the first is rarely the largest.

tips.title: Avoid Floodwater and Debris Fields
tips.body: Tsunami debris contains sharp objects, hazardous materials, and downed power lines. Treat all floodwater as contaminated.

tips.title: Document Damage Before Cleanup
tips.body: Photograph all damage for insurance purposes before moving or discarding debris. Contact your insurer and FEMA to begin assistance.

tips.title: Check Water and Food Safety
tips.body: Municipal water supplies may be compromised. Use bottled or boiled water until authorities confirm tap water is safe.

tips.title: Watch for Aftershocks
tips.body: If the tsunami was triggered by an earthquake, aftershocks will follow and could generate additional tsunami waves. Stay vigilant.
